record.text: AI health chatbots offer unreliable information. Studies show nearly half of responses are problematic & models struggle with initial diagnoses, often exceeding 80% failure. Verify AI outputs with a physician—don’t self-diagnose.

external.description:
Two newly published studies offer a flashing yellow light for anyone who searches for health answers on artificial intelligence chatbots.

One study found that essentially half of chatbot responses to health questions were "problematic," while another study found an 80% failure rate from AI models tested for their ability to replicate how a doctor would come up with the right list of possible conditions for a patient’s symptoms.

“If you're using these chatbots, show them to your physician, show what the outputs are, and make that a source of discussion,” said Arya Rao, a co-author of one of the studies. “Don't just rely on what's coming out of these tools.”

'Highly problematic'

Nick Tiller was the lead author on a study published last week that tested five widely used chatbots: Google’s Gemini, DeepSeek, Meta AI, ChatGPT, and Grok.

Tiller and his colleagues fed the chatbots questions related to cancer, vaccines, stem cells, nutrition and athletic performance.

And he said nearly half (49.6%) of responses were problematic, including nearly 20% that were deemed “highly problematic.”

Tiller said the highly problematic responses from AI had the potential to cause harm if people followed them.

People are sometimes self-diagnosing with AI instead of internet searches. But they may be simply trusting the AI summary in doing so, as opposed to clicking around to reputable websites to find an answer, Tiller said.

But he said users may not realize that the chatbots are usually just using statistical patterns to come up with answers based on their training data, often bypassing real-time information from the internet.

And a chatbot’s answers are only as good as its training data.

Tiller, a research associate at the Lundquist Institute for Biomedical Innovation at Harbor-UCLA Medical Center, said the researchers found Grok to be most likely to produce highly problematic responses, possibly because he said it's partially trained on social media content from X. Tiller called that a “cesspit of misinformation.”

“It has no capacity to make ethical judgments to weigh information,” he said of any chatbot’s process for coming up with a medical answer. “It's just predicting the most likely word in a sentence.”

Tiller said the team of researchers took an “adversarial approach” in questioning the chatbot, pushing the models to see if they’d spit back misinformation.

Tiller said they took that approach because it replicates how people ask health questions in the real world.

“They're not asking, ‘What is the scientific evidence behind vaccine safety?’ They're asking, ‘What are the risks of vaccines?’ or ‘How can vaccines harm me?’” he said of how people pose questions “slanted one way or another.”

Tiller specializes in nutrition and athletic performance. The paper’s co-authors were experts in various other fields covered in the audit of response quality.

Tiller said he understands why people turn to chatbots for health answers, given affordability and access challenges to health care.

But he urged caution in their use.

“Unfortunately, chatbots are not able to reliably provide you with accurate answers. Not yet,” he said.

“I'm really optimistic about the area,” he quickly added.

While Tiller doesn’t foresee AI replacing human doctors, he does see it rapidly improving and becoming a valuable supplement for health care professionals.

Diagnostic trouble

Another team of researchers tested 21 off-the-shelf AI models on 29 standardized clinical vignettes and found struggles at the “differential” stage of figuring out what’s wrong with a patient.

“The differential is a list of possible diagnoses that the physician will make when a patient first comes in, including common stuff, but also super rare stuff that you can't miss,” said one of the study’s authors, Dr. Marc Succi, a radiologist and the director of the MESH Incubator, an innovation and entrepreneurship center at Mass General Brigham and Harvard Medical School. “And that really is the art of medicine, is figuring out what that is. But you can't test for 100 different things, so you actually got to be very concise but comprehensive at the same time.”

Succi said the study was designed to reflect how medicine actually works in practice, not just whether AI can arrive at a correct final diagnosis with all the information in hand.

Across models, failure rates, the proportion of questions not answered fully correctly, were highest for differential diagnosis, often exceeding 80%.

The chatbots eventually performed better further along in the diagnostic process, but Rao, a Harvard medical student and the lead of the AI subgroup within the MESH Incubator, said many people interact with AI at the most uncertain stage of diagnosis, which is where the chatbots performed their worst.

Rao said they tested the AI systems with questions made for physicians. But they purposely picked tools anyone would use.

Succi said doctors can spot errors in the AI outputs, but patients might not. And he said that creates risk for the general public relying on chatbots for medical answers.

Succi and Rao said AI does not reason like a human clinician, and their study showed shortcomings in AI’s ability to provide people with the “second-doctor opinion” they may be seeking from their living room couch.

“So, we encourage caution,” Rao said.
external.title: Asking AI health questions? Use with caution, researchers say

“The differential is a list of possible diagnoses that the physician will make when a patient first comes in, including common stuff, but also super rare stuff that you can't miss,” said one of the study’s authors, Dr. Marc Succi, a radiologist and the director of the MESH Incubator, an innovation and entrepreneurship center at Mass General Brigham and Harvard Medical School. “And that really is the art of medicine, is figuring out what that is. But you can't test for 100 different things, so you actually got to be very concise but comprehensive at the same time.”

Succi said the study was designed to reflect how medicine actually works in practice, not just whether AI can arrive at a correct final diagnosis with all the information in hand.

Across models, failure rates, the proportion of questions not answered fully correctly, were highest for differential diagnosis, often exceeding 80%.

The chatbots eventually performed better further along in the diagnostic process, but Rao, a Harvard medical student and the lead of the AI subgroup within the MESH Incubator, said many people interact with AI at the most uncertain stage of diagnosis, which is where the chatbots performed their worst.

Rao said they tested the AI systems with questions made for physicians. But they purposely picked tools anyone would use.

Succi said doctors can spot errors in the AI outputs, but patients might not. And he said that creates risk for the general public relying on chatbots for medical answers.

Succi and Rao said AI does not reason like a human clinician, and their study showed shortcomings in AI’s ability to provide people with the “second-doctor opinion” they may be seeking from their living room couch.

“So, we encourage caution,” Rao said.
external.title: Asking AI health questions? Use with caution, researchers say

record.text: Authorities charged two and seek one suspect for a shooting on the Pennsylvania Turnpike. The group allegedly used flashing lights to impersonate police, then fired upon the occupants of a vehicle they stopped.

external.description:
Two men were arrested and another is still wanted in connection with a shooting on the Pennsylvania Turnpike on March 28.

Pennsylvania State Police say Tuften Green, 21, and Terrell Adams, 38, used flashing red and blue lights to impersonate a police vehicle and pull over a vehicle with three people, including a 3-year-old.

Green then shot the driver in the chest after they became suspicious, troopers said. Multiple other shots were fired as the victim's vehicle drove away.

Troopers said Adams, Green and Roberto Morel, 51, who trailed the fake police vehicle in another car, all fled from the scene.

Adams and Morel were arrested and taken into custody, but Green is still wanted by state police.

The trio face dozens of charges, including attempted murder, aggravated assault, robbery, kidnapping, and related offenses.

Adams and Morel were both denied bail "to ensure public safety," according to their criminal dockets. They are staying in Chester County Prison.

Anyone with information on Green's whereabouts are urged to contact state police at 610-486-6280.
external.title: 2 charged, 1 wanted after impersonating police during Pennsylvania Turnpike shooting

external.description:
The article highlights the enduring appeal of the Hobbiton film set in Matamata, New Zealand, originally created for The Lord of the Rings trilogy. More than two decades after the films were released, the location remains a fully preserved and carefully maintained tourist attraction. The piece emphasizes how the films succeeded in creating a believable and immersive world, largely due to detailed craftsmanship and New Zealand’s natural landscapes. The Shire, depicted as a peaceful countryside with rolling green hills and hobbit homes embedded into the earth, became one of the most iconic settings in modern cinema.

Today, visitors can experience this fictional world in real life through guided tours operated by Hobbiton Tours. The site features intricately designed set pieces, including hobbit holes, gardens, and pathways that closely resemble their on-screen appearance. The article suggests that the attention to detail and ongoing upkeep make the location feel authentic rather than a leftover film set.

In addition to describing the visual charm of Hobbiton, the article invites readers to reflect on the cultural impact of the films and their continued relevance. It also encourages those who have visited to share their experiences, reinforcing the destination’s popularity among fans and travelers alike. Accompanied by images, the piece focuses more on visual appreciation and atmosphere than logistical details, positioning Hobbiton as both a cinematic landmark and a must-see travel destination for fans of the franchise.
external.title: A Look at the Preserved Hobbiton Film Set and Tours in New Zealand

external.description: U.S. President Donald Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon have agreed to extend a temporary cease‑fire by three weeks, following high‑level talks at the White House between ambassadors from both countries and senior U.S. officials. The original 10‑day truce, brokered earlier in April 2026, had been set to expire on a Sunday and was intended to pause fighting between Israel and the Iran‑backed group Hezbollah in southern Lebanon. Trump stated on his Truth Social platform that the extension reflects progress in diplomatic efforts and that the United States plans to work closely with Lebanon to help it defend itself from Hezbollah. U.S. officials have underscored that the extended cease‑fire is meant to create breathing room for broader negotiations on security guarantees, the status of Israeli troops in southern Lebanon, and humanitarian measures such as prisoner releases and reconstruction. The move comes amid an ongoing regional conflict involving Iran, Hezbollah, Israel, and American forces, which has already seen several rounds of attacks and temporary truces. Diplomats and regional analysts warn that the agreement remains fragile, as both sides continue to build up military positions and maintain conditions for potential future hostilities, but the extension is being framed in Washington as a small step toward stabilizing the front and reducing civilian casualties in northern Israel and southern Lebanon.
external.title: Israel and Lebanon Agree to Three‑Week Extension of Cease‑Fire Amid Iran‑Led Conflict

record.text: Enterprise AI accounts are invoiced like any other product: sales tax is collected in the U.S. Does this mean that states might encourage AI use through policies to maximize token use? More tokens, more taxes! 😀

CSV files are plain text with a simple structure AI can parse instantly, while Excel files contain layers of metadata & formatting that create noise in the AI's interpretation. Claude and Copilot are prone to hallucinations & use substantially more tokens for Excel files (.xlsx) than for CSV files.

Our experience so far is that token savings from caching are not the norm. Our internal guidance has been to take a hybrid approach, some with Opus 4.6, some with Sonnet, and even some with Haiku when fast + dumb is acceptable. I have been advocating a "right tool for the job" approach.

external.description:
Improve user experience with a single Microsoft Copilot app to use on any given device. The new RemoveMicrosoftCopilotApp policy setting allows you to uninstall Copilot from devices in your organization in a non-disruptive way. It only applies to devices and users that meet the following conditions:

  * Microsoft 365 Copilot is also installed.
  * Copilot wasn’t installed by the user.
  * Copilot wasn’t launched in the last 28 days.



It’s available as a Policy CSP and Group Policy. **When will this happen:** The policy is broadly available as of April 14, 2026. It’s part of the April 2026 Windows security update and later. **How this will affect your organization:** This policy simplifies the user experience by providing a single Copilot app to use on any given device. **What you need to do to prepare:** No action is needed if your organization prefers the default experience and allows users to use Copilot by choice. For scenarios that meet the conditions and require you to remove Copilot from organizational devices, set this policy as described in Additional information. You’ll need at least the April 2026 Windows security update to get this policy. Users will still have the option to reinstall Copilot if they choose to. **Additional information:**

  * Learn more at WindowsAI Policy CSP.
  * Get the April 14, 2026—KB5083769 (OS Builds 26200.8246 and 26100.8246) security update.
external.title: MC1289507: New IT admin policy: Remove Microsoft Copilot app

record.bridgyOriginalText:
Improve user experience with a single Microsoft Copilot app to use on any given device. The new RemoveMicrosoftCopilotApp policy setting allows you to uninstall Copilot from devices in your organization in a non-disruptive way. It only applies to devices and users that meet the following conditions: 

  • Microsoft 365 Copilot is also installed.

external.description:
🚨

**Major Update:** This post contains a significant change that may impact your organisation.

**[Introduction]**

To support deeper analysis of Copilot agent adoption and usage across your organization, Microsoft will introduce a **new export capability** in the _Agent Dashboard_. This feature will allow eligible users to download **de‑identified Copilot agent usage data** for analysis, helping organizations track adoption trends, optimize licensing, and support AI transformation strategies, while maintaining existing privacy and access controls. User identifiers are removed from the output.

This feature will require at least**50 Microsoft 365 Copilot licenses** and will apply to the **Viva Insights web app**.

**[When this will happen]**

  * **Public Preview:** We will begin rolling out in**late May 2026** and expect to complete rollout by**early June 2026**.
  * **General Availability (Worldwide):** We will begin rolling out in**late July 2026** and expect to complete rollout by**late August 2026**.



**[How this affects your organization]**

_Who is affected_

  * **Agent Dashboard users with full company‑level data access** , including:
    * Senior leaders (as identified in Microsoft Entra ID)
    * Admin‑assigned company‑level users
    * Delegates with company‑level access
    * Global analysts and admins



 _**Note:** Group managers with data access limited to their own teams will not have access to this feature._

_What will happen_

  * Eligible users will be able to export **de‑identified, user‑level Copilot agent metrics and agent attributes** aggregated by user and week, for up to the **past six months**. User identifiers are removed from the output.
  * The **export will include multiple data tables** , such as:
    * **Copilot agent metadata** including agent name, creator type, and publishing source
    * **Copilot agent usage metrics** , including the number of responses generated
    * **Copilot agent credit usage and user retention metrics**
    * **People metadata** , including organization and function type
  * **By default, this feature will be ON for all eligible users.**
  * Admins will be able to **manage or disable the feature at the tenant or group level at any time using Viva Feature Access Management (VFAM):**
    * Module: Viva Insights
    * Feature: Agent Metrics Export
  * There will be **no impact to user workflows** unless the feature is accessed by eligible users.



_Screenshot - View of the Agent Dashboard:_

**[What you can do to prepare]**

  * **No action is required** to enable this feature.
  * If you do not want this feature enabled by default, you **can disable it at the tenant or group level** using **Viva Feature Access Management (VFAM)**.
  * Review which users have full company‑level access to the Agent Dashboard.
  * Communicate the upcoming change to eligible users so they understand the new export capability and its intended use.



**Learn more:**

  * Connect to the Agent Dashboard | Viva Insights | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn
  * Delegate access to the Copilot Dashboard, Agent Dashboard, and Copilot insights | Viva Insights | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn
  * Manage settings for the Microsoft Copilot Dashboard, Agent Dashboard, and Viva Insights web app | Viva Insights | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn
  * Using Entra ID data - Connect to the Microsoft Copilot Dashboard for Microsoft 365 customers | Viva Insights | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn
  * Viva features management - Control access to features in Microsoft 365 | Microsoft Viva | Microsoft Learn



**[Compliance considerations]**

No compliance considerations identified. Review as appropriate for your organization.

external.description:
🚨

**Major Update:** This post contains a significant change that may impact your organisation.

Microsoft is hosting two upcoming Ask Microsoft Anything (AMA) events focused on updating Secure Boot certificate updates across your Windows estate before certificate expirations begin in June 2026. These AMAs are designed to help you get clarity on what’s changing and how to plan for deployment across your environment. Bring your questions and get practical guidance directly from Microsoft experts. **When will this happen:** Secure Boot certificates on Windows devices begin expiring in June 2026. The upcoming AMAs will take place on April 23, 2026 and May 18, 2026. **What you need to do to prepare:** Review the Secure Boot playbook to understand requirements, affected scenarios, and available tooling, then add one or both upcoming AMAs to your calendar:

  * Secure Boot AMA – April 23, 2026, 8:00 AM PDT
  * Secure Boot AMA – May 18, 2026, 8:00 AM PDT



Post your questions in the comments section of each page early and often. If you don’t see the ability to post a comment, make sure you are signed in to the Microsoft Tech Community. **Additional information:** For continued guidance and updates, bookmark the Secure Boot playbook and https://aka.ms/GetSecureBoot.

record.text: Those who know, know #msignite

still thinking about the time I was pushed to use Microsoft Power Automate for some automation despite being an actual literal computer programmer, because Power Automate came with our organization's Microsoft subscription
These are collective issues and academia is responsible for tidying up its own house. It's our duty not to squander tax money which is earmarked for knowledge production and education, not Microsoft.
Teams is ghastly and nobody needs email as a platform, which Microsoft Outlook is, introducing tons of needles bugs and even serious issues.

Where it *is* interesting is where it knows about a class of solution that I don't, and wouldn't have found through search (because I didn't know its name). It gets there through the LLM from the handy wavy spec and other context 

Again, it may not be the right solution, but having it helps.

On the other hand, once you've got it properly contextualized, you can usually leave it to iterate until it has achieved what you want.

But if you watch it working the temptation to intervene as it goes off in ever decreasing circles is difficult to resist. But resist you must.

I agree. I've been doing a lot with Copilot CLI. If you are solving a well solved problem it looks like a miracle. If you give it a good solution to a hard problem, you often have to fight to get it to put the work in to implement it. It biases to the simplest thing rather than the best.

external.description:
The future of Gang of Dragon, the freshman effort from Sega veteran Toshihiro Nagoshi’s eponymous Nagoshi Studio, now appears more murky. As noticed by observers on Reddit, the studio has deleted its YouTube channel and all related videos. While the Gang of Dragon trailer, which made its debut at The Game Awards 2025, is still available on the award show’s dedicated channel, the various supplementary material regarding the developer from its own channel has now vanished into the internet ether. 

 

This all comes after recent reports that Nagoshi Studio is facing financial struggles. According to Bloomberg, the studio’s previous funding partner, the China-based tech giant NetEase, has backed out of further funding for the title--which is estimated to cost around $44 million to complete. This has essentially put the future of the game in limbo, with the original article’s reporting stating that while Nagoshi Studio is looking for funding partners, it has not reached any deals, and the current funding is due to run out in May--which, as of this writing, is just a week away.Continue Reading at GameSpot
external.title: Gang Of Dragon Developer Has Seemingly Erased Its YouTube Channel

images.alt:
Mit einem Visual gab der japanische  Verlag  Kodansha heute bekannt, dass der Comedy Manga »Megane, Tokidoki, Yankee-kun« von Naruki eine Anime-Adaption erhält. Weitere Informationen zur Produktion, Sprechercast und zum Starttermin sind bislang nicht bekannt.

»Glasses With a Chance of Delinquent« erscheint seit Januar 2021 im Bessatsu Friend Magazine von Kodansha und umfasst bislang 10 Bände. Seven Seas Entertainment veröffentlicht den Manga auf englisch, aktuell sind dort 5 Bände verfügbar. Eine digitale Version des Mangas erscheint bei Kodansha K MANGA.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Für Momose gibt es nur einen Wunsch: eine ruhige und unauffällige Schulzeit ohne Ärger. Doch dann begegnet sie Ichikura, der auf den ersten Blick wie ein harmloser, stiller Mitschüler wirkt. Hinter Brille und gelassener Fassade verbirgt sich allerdings eine wilde Vergangenheit als gefürchteter Problemfall. Obwohl er behauptet, mit diesem Kapitel abgeschlossen zu haben, flammt sein altes Temperament immer wieder auf.

external.description: Susumu Kitagawa of Kyoto University, joint winner of the 2025 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, has long advocated turning air itself into a resource through metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). His porous crystals can sort and capture CO2, water vapor and other molecules — putting Japan in direct conversation with players like Switzerland's Climeworks in the rapidly evolving direct-air-capture industry. Here is how a basic-science breakthrough from Kyoto could rewrite the economics of decarbonization.
external.title: MOF Revolution: Nobel Laureate Susumu Kitagawa's Vision to Turn Air Into a Resource Reshapes Carbon Capture
